content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 11, 1990


Mary L. Leach
527 South L Street
Lompoc, CA 93436

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-181

Dear Ms. Leach:

This letter is in response to your request for confirmation
of the telephone advice provided to you on February 27, 1990,
concerning your duties and responsibilities under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Because the facts in your letter differ from those provided during
our telephone conversation, we are unable to confirm the advice as
provided.

Your request for advice does not refer to a specific
governmental decision but rather seeks guidance regarding
disqualification issues arising from various decisions.
Therefore, we treat your letter as a request for informal as-
sistance pursuant to regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).²

QUESTION

Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require you
to disqualify yourself from participating in decisions regarding
(1) the rezoning of the Knoell property, (2) a building moratorium
on land designated R-2 and R-3, and (3) application of certain
ordinances?

CONCLUSION

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require you to
disqualify from participating in decisions which will have a
foreseeable and material effect on your financial interests which
is distinguishable from the effect upon the public generally.


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an official opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. I-90-181
Page 2

FACTS

You are a planning commissioner for the City of Lompoc. On
December 5, 1989, prior to your appointment to the planning com-
mission, the city council approved Mr. Michael Knoell's applica-
tion to rezone his lot from R-2 to PD for purposes of dividing the
land into three lots to be sold independently. On or about
December 17, 1989, the city suspended Mr. Knoell's permit on a
third building proposed for the lot which is already developed
with two homes.

On January 2, 1990, opponents of the rezoning, including
yourself, gave testimony to the city council to effect that
the rezoning was in violation of existing ordinances. The city
council initiated a moratorium on all R-2 building, referred the
rezoning application back to the planning commission for
reconsideration and referred certain ordinances to staff for
review and clarification.

Immediately after your appointment to the planning commis-
sion, the city council amended the moratorium on building to
include property zoned R-3. When the building moratorium was
scheduled for consideration by the planning commission, the city
attorney advised you that you might have a disqualifying conflict
of interest. You then called the Commission for advice.

In the course of our telephone conversation you advised me
that, prior to your appointment to the planning commission, you
had expressed your opposition to the project in the local
newspaper and before the city council. You were not compensated
for these efforts. I inquired whether you had any business or
real property interests which might require your disqualification
and you informed me that you did not. I then advised you that if
you had not received any payments for pursuing your opposition to
the rezoning, and if you did not have any economic interests that
would require your disqualification, you need not abstain from
participating in decisions regarding the personnel issue.
This advice was based on information available to me at the time
of our telephone conversation.3

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he or she knows or has

3
Your letter indicates that you have certain economic interests
in the city. Our advice to you was not based on and did not
consider those economic interests because the information was not
available to us at the time of our telephone conversation. We are
not addressing those specific interests at this time but we are
providing you with a discussion of the conflict-of-interest provi-
sions of the Act to assist you in the future to determine whether
or not you have a conflict of interest.

File No. I-90-181
Page 3

reason to know he or she has a financial interest. An official
has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef-
fect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
the official or a member of his immediate family, or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu-
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250)
or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

* * *

For purposes of this section, indirect invest-
ment or interest means any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, by an agent on behalf of a public of-
ficial, or by a business entity or trust in which
the official, the official's agents, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.

Section 87103(a) - (d).

By virtue of your position as a planning commissioner you are a
public official. (Section 82048.) Accordingly, you must
disqualify yourself from participating in any decision which will
have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on
your financial interests which is distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally.

Foreseeability

The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere possibil-
ity; however certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v. Downey
Community Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal. App. 3d 983, 989-991;
Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817; In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed).) The Act seeks to prevent more

File No. I-90-181
Page 4

than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow,
supra at 823.)

In order to determine whether the effect of a decision on
your financial interests is foreseeable, you must analyze the pos-
sible effect of the decision on your financial interests. For
example, you must consider whether approval or disapproval of an
application to rezone a certain parcel will result in an increase
or decrease of factors such as changes in traffic patterns,
increased density, or greater noise levels which may increase or
decrease the value of your financial interests. Even when you
determine that the effect of a decision is foreseeable, you need
only disqualify from participation when the effect of the decision
will be material, and such effect will be distinguishable from the
effect upon the public generally.

Materiality

The Commission has enacted regulations which provide
guidelines for determining when the effect of a decision is mate-
rial. When the real property in which the public official has an
interest is indirectly involved in a decision, the effect of the
decision is material if:

(a) (1) The real property in which the of-
ficial has an interest, or any part of that real
property, is located within a 300 foot radius of
the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision, un-
less the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official's real property interest.

* * *

(3) The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius which is the subject of the deci-
sion and the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of:

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest; ....

Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) and (3).

You have stated in your request for advice that you own a
home which is located within 2,500 feet of the parcel which is the
object of the rezoning decision. If your home were within 300
feet of the parcel under consideration by the planning commission,
your disqualification would be required unless you could
demonstrate that the decision would have no financial effect on
the value of your home. Conversely, because your home is located

File No. I-90-181
Page 5

outside a radius of 300 feet from the project but within a 2,500
foot radius, then disqualification is not required unless the
decision is likely to increase or decrease the value of your home
by $10,000 or more.

You have also indicated in your request for confirmation of
telephone advice that you own a small business in the City of
Dimmoc. When a business entity is indirectly involved in a deci-
sion, Regulation 18702.3 (copy enclosed) provides guidelines,
based on the financial size of the business entity, for determin-
ing when the effect of a decision is material. Because you have
indicated that your business is small, subdivision (g) of Regula-
tion 18702.3 probably describes the applicable standards which
would require disqualification if:

(1) The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more; or

(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

Regulation 18702.2(g), copy enclosed.

Clients of your business are considered sources of income to
you (Section 82030), and thus must be considered in analyzing your
duties under the Act. (See Section 87103.) If you are the sole
owner of your business, any client who has paid or promised $250
or more in gross revenues to your business during the past 12
months is considered a source of income totaling $250 or more to
you. If the decisions in question would foreseeably and materially affect any of those clients, you would be required to
disqualify yourself from participating in those decisions.

The facts you have provided indicate that no clients of your
business would be directly affected by the decisions in question.
We do not have sufficient information to determine whether the
indirect effects on any of your clients would be material and thus
would require your disqualification from any of the decisions in
question.

Public Generally

Even when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect of the decision on your financial interests is distinguish-
able from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.)
For city planning commissioners, the public consists of all
residents of the city. Thus, disqualification is required unless

File No. I-90-181
Page 6

the decision will affect your financial interests in substantially
the same manner as it will affect all residents of the city, or a
significant segment of the residents of the city. (Regulation
18703, copy enclosed.)

We have insufficient information to determine whether the
rezoning of the Kneell property, the building moratorium on land
designated R-2 and R-3, and the review and clarification of
certain ordinances will affect your financial interests in a man-
ner that is distinguishable from the effect on all other residents
of the city or a substantial number of those residents. In order
to make this determination, you must assess the consequences of
the decision, and then ascertain the effect of the decision on the
residents of Lompoc. For example, rezoning that provides for
increased population density results in increased pollution, traf-
fic congestion, and demand for services. In general, residents in
the immediate vicinity of high density areas feel the impact of
the increased population to a greater degree than those residents
of the city who reside farther away from the project. Once you
have determined the impact of a decision on all or a substantial
number of residents of the city, you are in a position to
determine whether the public generally exception applies to your
facts.

Enclosed please find a copy of the Political Reform Act and a
pamphlet entitled California's Conflict of Interest Law for Public
Officials. Should you have further questions regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, do not hesitate to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breeze
    Counsel, Legal Division

KED:BMB:plh

Enclosures
